## [00:00 - 03:05]

This is the pubcast shot. I'm John Loomer. Avoid the bottomless pit of despair. Let's go. So I get an email summary every morning from Reddit. That's probably a mistake that I have these scheduled for every morning because they're a really bad way to start my day. Without fail, the highlights are always something that feed into what I call the bottomless pit of despair. Now, this phenomenon doesn't only happen on Reddit and it's of course not unique to meta-advertising. But we're going to stay on topic today and you will find this on just about any other meta-advertising social community. So let me explain. It always starts with a post that is a plea for help. A headline about how someone's meta-advertising results tanked. They were getting good results and now they're not. They might even share how they tried various strategies and nothing worked. They used different performance goals. They created campaigns for different stages of the funnel. They used ad sets where Advantage Plus audience was on, others where it was off, others where they used suggestions, and others where they used remarketing. They tested ads in a separate campaign to find the best combination of copy and creative. And then this is where they'll blame meta, the bugs, the Advantage Plus enhancements, or straight up call meta-ads a scam. Now, do you notice what's missing here? We don't know any of the things that matter. We don't know the industry, the product, the price point, or the specific ad copy and creative strategy. We don't have examples of the ads or links to the landing page. And we'll come back to this. Now, by itself, this kind of social media post should not result in the bottomless pit of despair. Other elements are required. The most important element is validation. Now, this post should live on an island where everyone ignores it. But there's always a pool of people eager to agree. Yeah, my results tanked at the exact same time. Something's wrong. People will give them that dopamine rush by liking the post and sharing their agreement. They'll all come to the conclusion together that they're right and meta-advertising is a scam. So the reason that this is the bottomless pit of despair is that we have a very large group of people who are misguided and are looking to the wrong places for solutions. Understand that the core of all of this, what they are saying by not saying certain things,

## [03:05 - 06:06]

is that their industry doesn't matter. Their product, their price point, the ads themselves in the landing page don't matter. Only the tactics matter and they tried it all. If they set up their campaigns the way that some guru told them to set it up, they should get results. And if they don't get results from that tactic, they'll use another one and another one and another one. And after trying all of these guaranteed strategies and they're not getting results, or maybe they get results briefly before they disappeared, there's only one conclusion. Meta-ads don't work. This is a scam. It's a bottomless pit of despair because it requires an emphasis on things outside of your control and eliminates responsibility. It's a terrible place to be. So, we don't want to be in that pit. What should you do if you're not getting good results? First, make sure that you're not following any of the over-complicated tactics. There's nothing more than window dressing and a way for some guru to sell a course. If you're not getting results, the first step is to eliminate all of the unnecessary distractions that may actually make your results worse. Simplify by limiting your number of campaigns and ad sets. Focus only on conversions during this step especially. Use broad targeting. Limit customizations. Consolidate your budget to a limited number of assets. Now, you may eventually add complexity back. But this is the first step to understanding what is and isn't working. Your solution starts with the ads. No matter how good they are, they are never perfect. I guarantee it. They can always be improved. Look at your ads and ask, why aren't people acting on them? Covered this on an episode not too long ago. Are you considering your various customer personas? Their pain points? How your product is a solution to their pain? Are you following best practices? Are you using different formats like video, static image, even carousel? Are you providing multiple text options? Maybe five primary text and five headlines per ad? Do you customize by placement to be sure the right aspect ratio and format are used for each placement? And then did you use the advanced preview to confirm everything looks the way it should? And then look at the landing page. Does it load quickly or slowly? Is it clean and professional?

## [06:06 - 08:31]

Or is it outdated and cheap looking? Not just any landing page will do. Does a purchase require too many steps? Are the steps broken or confusing? These things will hurt your ads. And then confirm you've got everything set up properly for attribution. Your pixel, your conversions API, events, deduplication, you know the stuff. So here's the bottom of the glass. I don't want you in the bottomless pit of despair. It's no fun there. I want you in a place where you feel in control. You're not guaranteed to get good results. And there's no lever or strategy that will override a bad product or set of ads. When you're not getting the results you want, look at the things you can control.
